Yeah, I think you know, I was at RSA conference two weeks ago now at San Francisco, speaking with a lot of uh CSOs and leaders, and I think one of the main messages was that now completely risk-free AI inside the enterprise is not possible.
At least we don't have the solution today.
So it means that today you need to compute some sort of risk reward you know ratio, and and you need to be comfortable with a certain level of risk, known risk, right?
So you can you know with awareness go into a system where you're like, I know that this you know, five-10% part might go wrong, but but then you put the right control in place.
So if it does go wrong, you can just catch it very quickly and you can just roll it back.

And so I think right now the the new mentality is like not to go completely risk-free, but to have the right balance with your leadership, risk reward, what we are allowing the organization to push how far.
But uh, but that's the new the new mentality.
And the last thing I want to say about that is that the biggest cost and the biggest risk is not to do AI, because definitely things are accelerating, and I think that's not the question anymore.
And um and beyond the hype, we see real result, real RI, and the cost of not implementing that now is gonna be very, very expensive.
Yeah, yeah, I think you know it's the difference between policy setting and policy enforcement.
And I think today we have the opportunity to do policy enforcement by design.

And so you can really put in one place where you you design and craft the policy, then you can enforce it by design, and you can give this environment with the enforced policy, the enforced governance to your team, so they can already act and in uh you know in a sandbox where what they do it they're allowed to do.
And what they're trying to do, they cannot, they just cannot.
And so I think this is the the most powerful way to look at governance today.

And so we always have this typical um uh lifecycle where a customer starts to think about governance when they really feel the pain and the messiness that it gets when you start to unleash those systems without you know, some frameworks, some rules, some some sandbox that people can play into.

Some in the back end, some AI clients, some preferred cloud, some prefer Open AI, some uses codecs for for developers, and so the complexity with the number of agents, sometimes from third party vendors, sometimes from AI labs, the complexity really uh goes exponential, right?
Because you start to need to put governance at every different little space.

And so the huge advantage of actually centralizing your governance in one place and have one single point of entry for all your different agents is that they will all respect the same governance that you put in place only once.
And then you can deploy that to all your different agents and they will respect the same rules.
And if you have something that goes wrong, you can just revoke in one place and immediately it will be applied to all your AI stack.

So at first it's okay to experiment with you know ungoverned AI agents, just one, but then you will see that the complexity just goes exponential, and then if you don't centralize that, it becomes a crazy mess, crazy fast.
